solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public int maxVowels(String s, int k) {
        int maxVowels = 0;
        int currentVowels = 0;
        Set<Character> vowels = new HashSet<>(Arrays.asList('a', 'e', 'i', 'o', 'u'));

        for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
            if (vowels.contains(s.charAt(i))) {
                currentVowels++;
            }
            if (i >= k && vowels.contains(s.charAt(i - k))) {
                currentVowels--;
            }
            maxVowels = Math.max(maxVowels, currentVowels);
        }

        return maxVowels;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: This solution uses a sliding window approach to efficiently count vowels in substrings of length k. It maintains a running count of vowels in the current window and updates it as the window slides. The use of a set for vowels allows O(1) lookup time. The main strength is its linear time complexity, making it suitable for large input sizes. The weakness is that it only works for fixed substring lengths.
